text: They were removed after 7823 Mwhr of reactor operation during which the specimens were held at $645 \pm 10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for 4800 hr and accumulated a thermal dose of $1.3 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm $^2$ .
The specimens showed no evidence of corrosion; however, a carbon-rich layer, 1 to 2 mils in depth, was noted where the Hastelloy N and graphite were in contact.

The Molten Salt Reactor Experiment is a single region reactor that is fueled by a molten fluoride salt (65 LiF, 29.1 BeF $_2$ , 5 ZrF $_4$ , 0.9 UF $_4$ mole %), moderated by unclad graphite, and contained by Hastelloy N (Ni-16 Mo-7 Cr-4 Fe-0.05 C, wt %).

The details of the reactor design and construction can be found elsewhere. $^1$ We knew that the neutron environment would produce some changes in the two structural materials - graphite and Hastelloy N.
Cook and others, and the details have been reported previously. $^2$ The facility is shown pictorially and schematically in Fig.
Each stringer is about 62 in. long and consists of two Hastelloy N rods and a graphite section made up of various pieces that are joined by pinning and tongue-and-groove joints.
The Hastelloy N rod has periodic reduced sections 1 l/8 in. long × 1/8 in. in diameter and can be cut into small tensile specimens after it is removed from the reactor.
The assembled stringers fit into a perforated Hastelloy N basket that is inserted into an axial position about 3.6 in. from the core center line.
There is another surveillance facility for Hastelloy N located outside the core in a vertical position about 4.5 in. from the vessel.
These specimens are exposed to the cell environment $(\mathsf{N}_2 + 2 - 5\% \mathsf{O}_2)$ .
The specimens were put into the reactor as rods 62 in. long by $\frac{1}{4}$ in. in diameter with reduced sections $\frac{1}{8}$ in. in diameter by $\frac{1}{8}$ in. long.
The long rod was made in seven pieces and welded together to obtain the 62-in.-long rod.
After removal from the reactor, the rod was sawed into small specimens with a gage section $\frac{1}{8}$ in. long by $\frac{1}{8}$ in. in diameter.

If the total indicated runout of the gage section was greater than 0.002 in., the specimen was not used.
The materials were received in the mill annealed condition (1 hr at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).
They were given a further anneal of 2 hr at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ prior to insertion into the reactor and the control facility.
The specimens from the first group were in the reactor from September 8, 1965, to July 28, 1966.
The reactor had operated 0.0066 Mwhr when the specimens were inserted and 7823 Mwhr when they were removed.
They were at temperature for 4800 hr with the temperature range being $645 \pm 10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
However, the material was only exposed to salt for 2796 hr.
2 and 3 along with the axial location of each specimen.

The peak thermal dose, based on the $^{59}\mathrm{Co}(n,\gamma)^{60}\mathrm{Co}$ transmutation, was $1.3 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm $^2$ and the fast dose (>1.22 MeV), based on the $^{58}\mathrm{Ni}(n,p)^{58}\mathrm{Co}$ transmutation, was $3 \times 10^{19}$ neutrons/cm $^2$ .
The cross section used for the $^{59}\mathrm{Co}(n,\gamma)^{60}\mathrm{Co}$ transmutation was 22.25 barns, and that for the $^{58}\mathrm{Ni}(n,p)^{58}\mathrm{Co}$ transmutation was 0.1262 barns.
The temperature accuracy was $\pm 0.75\%$ , the guaranteed accuracy of the Chromel-P-Alumel thermocouples used.

The extensometer (mechanical and electrical portions) produced measurements that could be read to about $\pm 0.02\%$ strain; however, other factors (temperature changes in the cell, mechanical vibrations, etc.) probably combined to give an overall accuracy of $\pm 0.1\%$ strain.
This error and the thermocouple accuracy combine to give a temperature uncertainty of about $\pm 1\%$ .
The results of tensile tests on the control specimens of heat 5081 are given in Table 2.
4 for strain rates of 0.05 and $0.002\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ .
Several of the specimens were run at various strain rates over the range of 2 to $0.002\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ .
The results of these tests are also given in Table 2, and the fracture elongations are given in Fig.
The fracture elongation is very sensitive to strain rate at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , but relatively insensitive at higher and lower temperatures.
The results of the tests on the surveillance specimens from heat 5081 are summarized in Table 3.
The irradiated material is characterized by a sharp drop in ductility with increasing temperature above about $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The ductility is lower at a strain rate of $0.002\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ than at $0.05\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ , but the difference decreases with increasing temperature.
7 where the dependence of the ductility on strain rate is shown for several temperatures.
We have looked individually at the properties of the surveillance and control specimens of heat 5081; let us now compare these properties.
8 as a function of temperature.
The ultimate stress is reduced approximately $8\%$ up to about $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ where the reduction becomes greater with increasing temperature.
The total elongation at fracture for the irradiated material is lower by about $30\%$ at room temperature, but recovers to be almost equivalent at $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
As the temperature is increased above $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the ductility of the irradiated material drops to where it is only about $4\%$ of that of the unirradiated specimens.
At $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ the surveillance and control specimens have about the same ductility with only a slight decrease in the ratio with decreasing strain rate.
At $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ there is a sharp drop in the ratio at a strain rate of about $0.05 \, \mathrm{min}^{-1}$ .

The ratio is a bit confusing at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; its rise with decreasing strain rate is due to the fact that the ductility of the control specimens decreases more rapidly with decreasing strain rate than that of the surveillance specimens.
At 760 and $850^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ the ratio becomes quite small and its dependence on strain rate becomes less.

The main difference is that the ductility of heat 5085 is consistently lower up to about $500^{\circ}\text{C}$ .
Both heats of the surveillance specimens exhibit a reduction in ductility at a test temperature of $25^{\circ}\text{C}$ ; however, the reduction is greater for heat 5085.
The ductility of heat 5081 recovers some with increasing test temperature, but heat 5085 maintains its reduced ductility.
Above about $500^{\circ}\text{C}$ the ductility of the controls drops rapidly, but the ductility of the irradiated surveillance specimens decreases more rapidly.
Both heats have very similar ductilities at temperatures above $600^{\circ}\text{C}$ .

The properties of heat 5081 in several metallurgical conditions are given in Table 6.
The anneal for 2 hr at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ generally decreased the strength and increased the rupture strain over that of the as-received material.
One exception to this behavior is an increase in ultimate stress at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ due to the 2 hr anneal at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The exposure to molten salt at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for 4800 hr caused a decrease in the ductility and slight variations in the strength.
The tensile properties of heat 5085 in the unirradiated condition are given in Table 7.
The properties of this heat are not significantly different in the as-received condition and after annealing 2 hr at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The exposure to molten salt at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ caused a general decrease of 10 to $20\%$ in the rupture ductility, but the strength was not affected significantly.

These specimens were irradiated at $43^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and the ductilities at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ $(0.05\mathrm{min}^{-1})$ and $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ $(0.002\mathrm{min}^{-1})$ were 34 to 36 and 13 to $14\%$ , respectively.
When irradiated at an elevated temperature, this same heat had elongations at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ $(0.002\mathrm{min}^{-1})$ of 6.5 and $6.1\%$ .
Heats 5067 and 5085 showed somewhat better ductilities at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ $(0.002\mathrm{min}^{-1})$ with values of 9 to $10\%$ being obtained for irradiations at elevated temperatures.
The ductilities of the two surveillance heats at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ agree closely with those observed previously for heats 5085 and 5067.

The creep-rupture properties of heat 5085 were investigated in three conditions - as-received, annealed 2 hr at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and 2 hr at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ plus 4800 hr in molten salt (Table 10).

The rupture strain of the as-received material is improved by annealing at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , but the long aging treatment at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ brings about a slight reduction in the fracture ductility.
The pattern is the same as that for the ductility with the minimum strength being observed for the as-received material and the maximum for the material annealed 2 hr at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The properties (ductility and strength) of heat 5081 are superior to those of heat 5085.
However, a comparison of these data with those for the control specimens in Tables 9 and 10 shows that irradiation has significantly reduced the rupture life and ductility of both heats.

The results used in this comparison are given in Table 12.
The data for heat 5085 agree quite well with those obtained previously in the ORR for this same heat and several other MSRE heats.
However, the results for heat 5081 are slightly superior.
The data indicate a minimum ductility for a rupture life of 1 to 10 hr with ductility increasing with increasing rupture life.
A lower ductility of heat 5085 after irradiation in the MSRE is also indicated, although the data scatter will not permit this as an unequivocal conclusion.
The superior ductility of heat 5081 and the least ductility of heat 5065 (heat used for the top and bottom heads of MSRE) are clearly illustrated.

Since transients may occur in which the material is strained at some temperature other than the operating temperature $(650^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , we ran a series of tests to determine what effect such transients could have on the fracture strain at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The results of these tests are given in Table 13.
The specimens were strained at other temperatures and then quickly cooled or heated to $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and tested to failure.
Even though the pretraining treatments carried the specimens as far as $37\%$ of the strain to failure, the strain at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was not decreased.
In fact, the pretraining at 760 and $850^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ seemed to improve the ability of the material to strain at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Figure 24 shows the microstructure of a control specimen from heat 5081 that was tested at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Although the micrographs that have been shown are for heat 5085, the surface reaction was also noted in heat 5081.

Electron microprobe examination showed that this layer contained from 0.3 to 1.5 wt % C, indicating that there was some transfer of carbon from the graphite to the Hastelloy N where the two materials were in contact.
Figure 33 shows the fracture of the surveillance specimen from heat 5085 that was tested at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
This technique involves depositing carbon on a polished surface of the specimen and then dissolving electrolytically the matrix in a solution of $10\%$ HCl in ethanol until the carbon falls free.

The control specimens from both heats of Hastelloy N exhibited the characteristic ductility minimum in the temperature range of 600 to $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The ductility at various temperatures was reasonably insensitive to strain rate except near $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ where the ductility decreased with decreasing strain rate.

At elevated temperatures (400-850°C) the fracture strains were equivalent for the two heats of surveillance specimens and were found to be the same as those observed previously in other experiments where molten salt was not present.

Creep-rupture tests run on unirradiated specimens indicated that the pretest anneal of 2 hr at $900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ improved the rupture strength and ductility over that of the material in the as-received condition.
Thus, the stress-relieving anneals at $871^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ used in fabricating the MSRE improved the base properties.
However, the long exposure of the control specimens at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (operating temperature of the MSRE) caused slight reductions in rupture strength and ductility.
Heat 5085 was affected much as we had observed previously in other experiments on this same heat and other MSRE heats.
Heat 5081 exhibited a significant but smaller effect.
The creep-rupture ductility of irradiated Hastelloy N goes through a minimum value for a rupture life of from 1 to 10 hr.
Heat 5081 exhibited appreciably higher fracture strains

A series of tensile tests indicated that prestraining at temperatures over the range of 400 to $850^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ did not appreciably lower the fracture strain of the material when tested to failure at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
However, there was a surface product from 0.001 to 0.002 in. in depth that formed where the Hastelloy N and graphite were in contact.

The changes in the unirradiated properties of these heats of Hastelloy N have been demonstrated previously.7 However, these changes are relatively small, and the properties do not appear to be deteriorating enough for concern.
They are probably due to the solution and reprecipitation of $\mathbf{M}_{6}\mathbf{C}$ .

Previous work had shown a reduction in the rupture ductility in postirradiation tensile and creep-rupture tests at elevated temperatures.[8,9] These effects are attributed to the helium produced by the $^{10}\mathrm{B}(n,\alpha)$ transmutation.
Many of the details of the role of helium in metals have been reviewed by Harries,[10] and we have applied these
ideas to Hastelloy N in two-recent papers. $^{11,12}$ Hence, we shall not discuss the mechanism whereby helium alters the properties.
However, about 30 to $40\%$ of the $^{10}\mathrm{B}$ was transmuted in the surveillance specimens with a resulting atomic fraction of helium of about $1 \times 10^{-5}$ .

Hence, the exact helium level is rather academic, and the high-temperature properties have probably stabilized unless continued exposure at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ causes metallurgical changes that are detrimental.

They were removed after 7800 Mwhr of operation during which the specimens were held at $645 \pm 10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for 4800 hr and accumulated a thermal dose of $1.3 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm $^2$ .
Two heats of material, 5081 and 5085, were used with both showing similar deterioration of tensile ductility at elevated temperatures.
However, heat 5085 (cylindrical core vessel) exhibited a greater loss of creep-rupture strength and ductility.
There was a significant reduction in the low-temperature ductility, but fracture strains of 35 to $40\%$ were still obtained.
This change in properties is probably due to the formation of intergranular $\mathbf{M}_{6}\mathbf{C}$ .
This layer was only 1 to 2 mils deep and probably would have little effect on the mechanical properties of thick sections.
